text: So what we see here are many distended loops of small bowel full of blood.
We have some adhesions to the anterior abdominal wall.
And this is the gastrojejunostomy that's just plastered to the undersurface of the liver.
So to begin, we're going to mobilize all of our important structures.
Here we're starting to mobilize the gastric pouch away from its omental attachments and later from any surrounding adhesions.
And the next step is then to dissect and separate the gastrojejunostomy from the liver.
To do this, we used electrocautery to dissect the plane between the left lobe of the liver and the gastrojejunostomy.
As you can see, it was a difficult dissection, lots of inflammation, all secondary to the marginal ulcer.
Once this is done, or once this was done, we then went on to see if we could divide or dissect the gastrojejunostomy away from the gastric remnant.
And as you'll see shortly, as we're doing this, we're coming across the ulcer itself.
So on screen left, you have the roux limb, and on screen right, we're pulling the gastric remnant.
And then the sucker's just going into the ulcer there.
So here we're just hugging the greater curve of the stomach.
And we continue to mobilize the gastric remnant before dividing it at the level of the antrum.
So once this is done, we're choosing our area of division, and it was in the antrum.
We used our linear staplers, and we had to use two firings to get across.
And once the antrum was divided, the antrum of the gastric remnant, the next step was then to resize the gastric pouch, which we're doing now.
While doing so, we resect the gastrojejunostomy, and then we then go on to make our gastric pouch smaller.
So here's our new gastric pouch right there.
And so now we've mobilized most of our specimen which would include our proximal gastric remnant as well as the gastrojejunostomy.
And we now just have to make our distal transection along the roux limb.
So we're finding our point of transection on the roux limb here.
We'll divide it and now our specimen will be completely resected on block.
So we're pushing the specimen cephalad so that we can turn our attention to forming our new gastrojejunostomy.
So here's the new proximal end of our roux limb.
We're bringing it up to the new gastric pouch and we are forming our new gastrojejunostomy.
We do this with a linear stapler and we close our common enterotomy with an absorbable suture and we do this in two layers.
For all our gastrojejunostomies, we do both an air leak test as well as a dye test.
In this video, we'll just show you the air leak test.
And here's our air leak test, and we're happy with our anastomosis.
And we're just finishing off our second layer.
So we're placing our specimen into our retrieval bag, which will be removed or was removed through our 12 millimeter port.
